You take hold of Karrah’s hand, and stare at her across the table. She looks determined, baring her teeth, her brow furrowed beneath her mohawk. The zaika gathered around you two seem almost tense, waiting to see who comes out ahead in this last round.
Next

You fix your gaze on Karrah, and start to grin at her as you push. A strained laugh escapes you, and just for a second, you see confusion flicker across her green face. She doesn’t know why you’re laughing. You don’t either, but you push harder, willing to take the advantage.
Next
You tell Karrah you’ll talk to her later. She gives you a grunt and a nod.
Next

You walk over to the horseshoe-shaped bar, heading for the half that the purple-haired kaithrit woman is working. The bar itself looks like it was cobbled together from spare parts from a ship lounge, like someone hacked a dozen different pieces of furniture apart and somehow put it all together as one piece and made it work. The top of it is made of several tables cut into pieces and bolted together, and it’s splattered with dozens of small drops of various colors, along with rings of liquid where drinks once sat.
Pretty sure i’ve seen you in here before too.
Got a name?”
the soldier of fortune type, though more fortune than soldier
one of those techies who can hack anything with a few wires and a spare bolt
this isn’t the first underground bar you’ve swaggered into
you’re a little distracted, one foot in this world and another somewhere else
, am I right?”

You walk over to the bar where Monika’s working, and pull up a stool. The cobbled-together bar looks the same as always, with the drips from various drinks staining the various panels that make up its surface. Monika’s pouring a few drinks for a zaika couple, filling glasses on a tray that looks like it used to be a panel from a ship’s wall. Once the drinks are full, she slides the tray over, then turns to you and picks up a shot glass as the zaika head for their table.

“Back again? You must like us, [pc.name], the way you keep showing up here,” she says, a small smile on her face. She spins the shot glass between her fingers, then makes it dance over the back of her hand. “Or you just like the booze, but that means you’ve got good taste either way. So what’ll it be?”

“[pc.name], good to see you,” she says with a familiar, friendly smile. She pulls out two shot glasses, then a third, and starts to juggle them with one hand, tossing one at a time into the air and then another before catching the first, sending them spinning in a circle. “What can I get for one of my favorite rushers?”

original: Monika’s a kaithrit, and stands a little over six feet tall, with light skin and a toned figure with some smooth curves. Her hair is purple, matching her two tails, and hangs down past her shoulders, held back by a black-and-white striped bandanna. She has one green eye that looks to be the one she was born with, and one cybernetic eye that’s a solid blue, with a metal rim around it and more metal in horizontal lines that sweep back toward her temple. A scar runs from her cybernetic eye all the way down to her jawline. The look she gives you, with both eyes, gives the impression that she’s constantly watching and analyzing everything and everyone around her.

You tell Monika you’d like a drink. She gives you a warm smile that looks like she’s practiced it a lot, and gestures to the booze displayed behind her. The bottles cover a range of different shapes, colors, and sizes, but it’s all cream liquor, as you saw before. The labels are mostly large text in a few different languages, along with pastoral scenes or symbols you can’t quite make out.

When you clear the outer atmosphere and descend through thick, pollution-blacked clouds toward a city-sized tarmac, the mystery of corporate paralysis becomes clear: Dhaal is already civilized! The twilight-draped skyline is dominated by towering highrises and monolithic structures worthy of any presidential palace. Galactic corporations typically use the Planet Rush to settle primitive worlds, so the prospect of one ripe for immediate commerce must have them falling over one another to gobble it up.

Following the archived coordinates you received brings you to a region of backwater space so far from any civilization that at first you’re worried there was some sort of glitch. As you draw closer, your sensors begin spitting out reassurances and your main screen beeps with a magnified visual.
Lights flash and flicker.
The pavement hits your back.
Ow.

You can be sure it’s not going to kill you, but that moonshine is already rotting your innards. You stumble over every thought, seriously wondering if you need an emergency airlift to [pc_ship.theName]. The smooth enervation of alcoholism settles in, makes everything woozy, and takes your mind off petty concerns. One drink and you’re already shlurring...

Everything is vague and warped; you’re incapable of focusing, yet the sounds, the lights, the noise pollution hits you, and you ripple like a disturbed pool. You don’t even notice yourself drifting from the table, a hollow thing that any force could bend to their will.
Better not have that again. Yes, it had a decent aftertashte but...
While the aftertaste was good, something tells you that you’d better not overindulge on that. Who knows what might happen to mentally absent [pc.raceShort] in a place like this?

Whatever it is, it is without a doubt one of the strongest drinks you’ve had. Nothing could drown out the taste of alcohol. It burns going down in that numbing, intoxicating way. Good to start with, but your liver is going to be pissed off later. It’s stale and tasteless, yet there’s something you can’t quite identify in the aftermath. Whatever it is, you don’t like it.

After getting the last drop down, you just feel... nothing. Inebriated, yes, but that’s about it. Your [pc.belly] churns, however, and your [pc.skin] goosebumps; you feel it just as clearly as if you had just stepped out of the cold and into the warmth. No ill effects, other than needing a good lie down. Or a fuck.

While the massive city that stretches as far north and south as your equipment can read, you are directed towards an open landing site that appears to be a work in progress. Huge industrial machines toil to bulldoze and pave as much of their world as needed, adding more parking for the off-world visitors. Automated shuttles zip from one craft to the next, their flickering signs inviting you to disembark for transportation to some place called the Gyre.

A mere moment after you start flagging, a gunmetal gray hovercraft zips over to you and pops its hatch, allowing you to climb into a bus-like back crammed with fellow Rushers. The woman in the driver’s seat, though, is of a race you’ve never seen before: she’s humanoid, a bit over five feet tall and slender, with dusk-hued purple skin and almond-shaped blue eyes. A messy mane of silver hair falls around her shoulders, breached on either side by a pair of elfin ears. Indeed, you’d easily mistake her for some kind of fantasy-game elf if it wasn’t for the long, alien tail running out of a hole in the back of her cargo shorts and flicking around on the floor. The tip’s covered in some kind of thick sock, shielding it from view, but it seems much more bulbous than the rest of the appendage.

“First time, huh?” the woman quips, throwing an arm over the back of her seat. “In that case, welcome to sunny, scenic Dhaal! I’m a zaika, and we’re the dominant species. Nearly as advanced as any of you alien types, don’t doubt it for a second. Be happy that Paragon Inc. has your best interest at heart; a lot of my sisters down at street level aren’t as happy as we are that there’s a whole galaxy coming down on our little slice of Dhaal all of a sudden.”

Your Codex quite unhelpfully chirps, “Zaika detected! This friendly species is on track for rapid integration into the United Galactic Confederacy, though 4,388 different travel advisories have been issued advising lactating species to explore elsewhere.”

That’s... encouraging. By now the other passengers - a motley mix of rugged Rushers from several species - are grumbling about the delay, so your native driver motions for you to sit and strap in. You barely have the time to comply before the shuttle shudders underfoot and starts careening through the air towards the sprawl of the city. Whatever that “Gyre” place is, it seems you’re enroute now!

You stand in the shadows of stratospheric buildings, lit by flickering neon advertisements and pale glows from the windows. The twilight sky is all but invisible unless you stare straight up in the mist of pollutants bearing down on you, drooling thick drops of rain down onto the narrow road you’ve been deposited on.

A pretty large gang of other zaika are heading up the road towards you, maybe a dozen of them in various torn-up leather outfits and black coats. Either they’re an all-female race or the males are vanishingly rare, because this particular gang are all various shades of buxom purple alien babe.
“Ah, shit, gangers already?” your driver complains, ratcheting a lever next to her seat. “Better make yourselves scarce, aliens, unless you want trouble!”

Armor plates fall down over the viewports as the last of the Rushers disembarks, and your fellow passengers quickly scatter down alleys and back streets before the gang overtakes you. You turn to leave as well, but just in time to hear a loud ka-bang! from the back of the hovercar. Black smoke billows out of the grav-plating under it and from the engines, followed by a string of cursing from the driver as the hatch closes back up.

Almost as soon as they’ve started, you hear a siren wailing somewhere nearby. A few seconds later and a bulky armored truck colored the same silver-and-blue of the shuttle barrels down the road, skids to a stop a few yards off, and disgorges a group of armored zaika women wielding truncheons and shields. All of them have the word “PARAGON” emblazoned over their backs and shoulders. They form up into a shield wall and advance, as a voice booms from speakers on the carrier: “YOU ARE IN VIOLATION OF ZAIBATSU ACCORDS. CEASE DAMAGING PARAGON PROPERTY. DISPERSE NOW.”

It’s a short, brutal melee as the police-looking aliens wade into the crowd, dragging them off the hover-car and batting them around with their clubs. The Paragon troops have so much armor that it’s no contest between them, shrugging off the worst the mob can throw at them until the last members are being dragged down and cuffed.

Still and all, as you step up to the desk she greets you with a pretty smile and a wiggle of her elfin ears. “Greetings new customer! Welcome to the Paragon Capsule Resort - the paragon of comfort for any budget!”

She recites the canned line cheerily, tapping a button on her desk so that a menu of available rooms and services appears behind her. “I am Myssah, the manager of this establishment. Whenever you’re ready to rest and relax here at the resort, please don’t hesitate to let me know.”

She taps a button under her desk, and the resort’s menu appears holographically behind her. “Our capsules are economically priced,” she says, leaning forward over the desk in a way that makes all the leather she’s wearing creak, “but perhaps I could interest you in a full service experience?”
Great. Back out into the dangerous streets, then.

“Very well,” Myssah answers, crisp and professional. She turns an old-fashioned touch-screen display around to face you, showing an agreement and cost that you quickly scan over before swiping your Codex over a recently-added scanner bolted onto the side. The transaction completes, and Myssah smiles and hands you a plastic keycard from under the desk.

You head on back, using your keycard to unlock the door and head through into a dark chamber. When your eyes adjust to it, you see a vast, sprawling narrow row of three-foot hatches in the wall stacked twenty feet high. You quickly walk through the stacks towards the clearly-marked shower room. There’s another door inside the showers marked private, but your card only gets you access to the main, open showers - luckily for you it’s empty at the moment, and you’re able to get cleaned up fairly quick before trying to find your sleeping arrangements.

You end up climbing up to one of the hatches, using the handles on other units to heft yourself up to your own. It opens with a swipe of your keycard, and with some effort, you’re able to swing yourself into the hatch and squeeze inside. The capsule is barely big enough for you to fit inside it, with just enough room to lay down and nestle your head into the cheap pillow.

It’s hardly the most comfortable sleep you’ve ever had - there’s no room to stretch out, and barely enough clearance just to roll over, like the hotel picked a capsule the perfect size to sardine-ify you - but you are able to sleep eventually. It’s blessedly quiet and the air is cleaner than outside by far, plus with the cannons outside, you don’t need to worry about the street gangs barging in. It’s a momentary illusion of safety and security, if not necessarily comfort, here on Dhaal, and you’re happy to take what little is offered.

The planet is a strange sight. Half of the world is frozen in a jet black ice, seemingly as lifeless as the void, while the other half bakes in the molten heat of its star. The readout explains that Dhaal is tidally locked: the planet’s rotation matches its orbit in such a way that the far side never sees day and the near side never sees night. The only part of the planet on which life can exist at all is a band running from the north pole to the south and back again, perfectly situated between the unforgiving extremes and bathed in an eternal twilight.

At first, it appears as if the planet has a thin, glittering ring orbiting it. This illusion is dispelled by the chatter of hundreds of signals filling your scanners with buzzing babble. There are so many ships in the planet’s thermosphere that they fill the darkness with a halo of twinkling lights. The easily recognizable logos of various MegaCorps are brightly branded across the hulls; galactic civilization impatiently waits for the go-ahead to descend on Dhaal like vultures.
(Gyre’s end taxi stand added to taxi destinations!)
It looks safe inside... but the streets winding away to north and west still tempt with dangerous unknowns.
You descend slowly, hands ready on your weapons, just in case.

When you reach the landing, an underground area opens up around you. It’s a single long chamber, big enough to hold a small starship, with a long metal track running down the center. Most of the floor is covered with gray dust. Piles of rubble sit on either end of the track, blocking off what looks like a tunnel. Lights flicker on the domed ceiling, growing brighter and dimmer in uneven waves, making the whole place seem hazy and dangerous.

Graffiti covers the walls, crude drawings of zaika girls and giant sex organs and anti-zaibatsu scribbles. High up on one wall, you can make out faded lettering, spelling out “yre Station”. It looks like this was supposed to be part of some kind of underground rail system, but there’s no train.

There’s a door beneath the lettering, a giant piece of riveted metal that looks like someone pulled it off a spaceship and jammed it onto the wall. It doesn’t match anything else down here, almost conspicuously out of place. There’s no knob or handle on the door, and more of the yellow lights flicker around its frame. Is this where the graffiti arrows on the street above were pointing?

You look at the graffiti some more, then spot what you’re looking for – an arrow done in the same curling style, with “moo” written beneath it, pointing at the metal door. It blends in so well with the rest of the crude drawings, you wouldn’t have seen it if you didn’t know what to look for.
Might as well find out what’s down here, since you’ve come this far. You approach the giant steel door, but before you can get close enough to knock, harsh lights around it flare to life.

A blue hardlight projection appears in front of the door, a sneering thraggen woman standing at seven feet tall. Her scar-striped arms are folded over a pair of tits big enough to lose your entire head between, barely kept in place by an armored vest that’s way too small for her and leaves her thick, muscular arms free. Armored pants and heavy boots complete her ensemble, and she has a stun baton and some kind of large pistol at her belt. A sleek mechanical cannon is mounted on her left shoulder, pointing down at you, and she wears a cybernetic monocle over her left eye. A tattoo of the letters “RR” marks her right shoulder. Her hair is spiked up in an aggressive mohawk, her fangs are jutting up from behind her lower lip, and she does not look happy to see you.
“New meat,” the thraggen growls. “The fuck do you want?”

You tell her you saw the graffiti pointing to this place, and you wanted to know what’s down here. Sure, she’s a hardlight projection, but she’s also clearly meant to be a threat. Whatever’s behind that door, whoever’s guarding it, they wouldn’t be using this image if they weren’t trying to hide something. And that cannon on her shoulder makes it clear they’re willing to shoot you if you don’t say the right thing.
The thraggen curls her upper lip in what’s either a smirk or a sneer. “Thought so,” she says. “The Gyre’s a dangerous place to be curious.”
You point out that you’re a rusher, and going to dangerous places and being curious about what’s there is what you do. No need to say more than that.

She grunts. “Got me there. You with any of the corps? The zaibatsu paying your ass?” She looks you up and down, and the cannon on her shoulder tilts to follow her gaze. “You don’t look like one of them, but you’re damn sure not hurting for creds.”
No, you’re not with any of the local corps. Again, you leave it at that. This doesn’t look like a good place to talk about who you really are.

“Let’s find out.” The thraggen reaches out to the side, and her hand disappears from the hardlight projection for a moment before reappearing. So there’s a real person behind this, somewhere. “Facial scan’s clean.” She snorts. “Okay, meat. Congratulations on finding the Crash Landing. Listen up and I’ll tell you how things work down here.”
After the welcome you got when you arrived on Dhaal, a drink might be a really good idea. And if it’s not under the corporations’ control, so much the better.
“Good,” the thraggen says, then gives you a look like she’s about to bite your throat out. “You want in, you agree to the rules. Listen up.”

“First up: you don’t touch the girls unless they say so. We’ve got a fucking genius techie who rigged up a system here to stun the living shit out of you from anywhere in the bar if you put one finger where it doesn’t belong. Then we toss you out for the milk thieves. And if you’re real bad, before we do that, I chop off your hand and pin it on my wall. Got it?”
Sounds like a rough place, but you know how to keep your hands to yourself. What’s the second rule?

“Second: you hold your milk, or your booze, or whatever gets you off. People are here for a good time in this shithole city, not to watch you make an ass of yourself. Keep it together or we throw you out and you don’t get to come back.”
You’re not in the habit of making a fool of yourself at bars anyway.

“I was doing that, wasn’t I,” she says, half to herself. “So yeah, you’re not wrong about me being an army brat. That was life for us, around the core worlds. Both my parents served, and they pulled strings to make sure they got assigned together, so we hopped from one planet to another every year or two.”
That doesn’t sound like too bad of a life. Growing up in the core must have been interesting, even if she kept moving around.

“It had its perks,” she says, then gestures to the bar. “Makes living life out here on the fringe seem real weird. But it was the only thing I knew. And my parents made army life sound good, I signed up soon as I was old enough.”

So she’s former military? That’s a little bit of a surprise. Though you bet lots of pirates start that way, then decide to be their own bosses once they’ve got the training and experience to handle that life.

“That happens, though mostly you get old soldiers sticking around way too long,” she says, then picks up a shot glass and starts toying with it. “But when I was younger, I thought it was the greatest thing ever. I must have been the only kid in boot camp who jumped right out of bed soon as the alarm went off, sleeping in boots that were already polished.” She grins, though it looks strained. “I was kind of a little shit, now that I look back at it.”
Growing up military, though, that makes sense. Especially if her parents made it seem like a great life.

“They did,” Monika says, “and I believed all of it. I wanted to serve the UGC, which acts like it’s the only real reason we’ve got a galactic civilization at all.” She spins the shot glass around two fingers, then makes it disappear. “I held onto that for a long time. Probably too long.”

So she got disenchanted with the military. That happens to a lot of people, or so you’ve heard. And if she grew up with glory stories and saw how it really was, you can see how she’d come to change her mind.

“You’re not wrong.” Monika makes the shot glass reappear, then stares into it for a moment before stowing it below the bar again. She looks at you, her expression unreadable. “But that’s a story for some other time.”

Well, she’s still alive to tell it, so it couldn’t have been that bad. Could it?
“Bad enough.” Monika leans one hip against the bar, moving in closer to you. When she speaks again, her voice is low.

“My parents never told me that most of the UGC’s job in the core worlds is keeping people from fighting back. They don’t have enough of an army for the whole damn galaxy, so a lot of worlds never see troops at all. I started to catch on when I realized I’d spent four years in a big, long lap around the galactic core.”
If she was keeping people from fighting back, does that mean putting down rebellions, or dealing with protests, or what?

“Both of those and more,” she says, her voice still low. “I thought I’d get to see strange new worlds and conquer them for the UGC, but most of our work was on people we never should have had to fight.” She looks away, and her ears lower, like she’s remembering something she’d rather keep buried. “I should have gotten out sooner. But I signed up for a five-year term, and there was no way I’d walk out of that. My parents would have disowned me.”
You decide not to ask if her parents know she went space pirate, and simply wait for her to continue.

“Turns out, I didn’t have to worry about that. I had about seven months left when I took a frag grenade to the back.” Her tails rub up against her back, and you’re not sure if she’s doing that on purpose or not. “Even armored, that wasn’t something I could walk away from. And that explains the scars all over her back.

“Two words: honorable discharge,” she says, and gives you a smile that doesn’t reach her eyes. “Not that it could have gone any other way. Someone made up a story about me diving in front of a bomb to save my squadron, but the truth is I just didn’t see it coming. I walked out of there with a shit paycheck and a ‘sorry about the scars’. Didn’t even serve long enough for a commendation I could use to get a job.”
That’s pretty brutal. She could have died, and they didn’t give her much when they let her go just because she hadn’t been there long enough?

Monika shrugs. “That’s the UGC. But it worked out in the end.” She gestures to the bar again, then smiles, and it looks genuine this time. “There I was, fully trained and covered with scars, no future ahead of me and a past I wanted nothing to do with.” She smirks. “Seemed like a damn good time to become a pirate.”
